content:
journald, nginx logs, and the df -h check that saves you from debugging fake app bugs.

3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When /var/log fills the root disk, services fail with "no space left on device" even though your app and database volumes have space. Free space fast with journalctl --vacuum-size=500M, then find the largest log directories with du -xh /var/log | sort -h | tail -10.
I have seen teams restart APIs, scale Kubernetes, and blame a deploy — while the real issue was 47 GB in journald from debug logging left on for two days.
Confirm disk full (not memory, not inode)
df -h /
df -i /100% on / with inodes fine → log or package bloat on root volume.
du -xh /var/log 2>/dev/null | sort -h | tail -15Typical offenders:
→ /var/log/journal/ — systemd journal → /var/log/nginx/ — access logs without rotation → /var/log/syslog, /var/log/auth.log — Debian/Ubuntu → /var/log/audit/ — auditd verbose rules
Step 1: Vacuum journald (fastest win)
journalctl --disk-usageExample output: Archived and active journals take up 47.2G.
Immediate relief:
sudo journalctl --vacuum-size=500MOr keep last 3 days:
sudo journalctl --vacuum-time=3dRuns in seconds. Often frees tens of GB.
Persist limits in /etc/systemd/journald.conf:
[Journal]
SystemMaxUse=1G
RuntimeMaxUse=200MThen:
sudo systemctl restart systemd-journaldStep 2: Rotate and truncate large text logs
Find big files:
sudo find /var/log -type f -size +100M -exec ls -lh {} \;Truncate (keeps file handle open for running daemons):
sudo truncate -s 0 /var/log/nginx/access.logOr rotate properly:
sudo logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/nginxCheck logrotate config exists for your app — many custom services have none.
Step 3: Clean package manager cache (if root is tight)
sudo apt clean # Debian/Ubuntu
sudo yum clean all # RHEL older
sudo dnf clean all # Fedora/RHEL 8+Usually smaller than journald but worth doing on small root volumes.
Step 4: Verify services recover
df -h /
sudo systemctl restart <failed-service>
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:8080/healthOn Kubernetes nodes, full root disk breaks kubelet sandbox creation:
journalctl -u kubelet --since "1 hour ago" | grep -i "no space"Free disk → kubelet recovers without node reboot in most cases.
Find what filled disk (so it does not repeat)
Who wrote to journal today:
journalctl --since today | wc -l
journalctl -u <service-name> --since "24 hours ago" | wc -lDebug logging left on after a ticket is the #1 cause I see. Grep your deploy for log level changes:
grep -r "DEBUG\|trace" /etc/your-app/Set production default to INFO or WARN. Structured logs at debug volume will fill any disk.
For a full production runbook — high load vs disk full vs OOM vs SSH lockout — Linux War Room has the diagnosis tree and emergency commands in order.
Day-to-day terminal workflows (disk, ports, processes) live in Linux Commands for Developers if you want a desk reference beyond this one incident.
Prevention checklist

• Alert when root FS > 85% — not only data volumes

• SystemMaxUse on journald on every server and node

• logrotate for nginx, app logs, and sidecars

• Never enable debug logging in prod without a TTL reminder

• df -h in weekly on-call runbook — boring, effective
Disk full looks like an application bug. Run df -h before you open the codebase. It has saved me more hours than any profiler.
